chunk_summary: Acute wasting arises from infection-driven catabolism, gut dysfunction, and inadequate intake, requiring energy supplementation, WASH interventions, and close follow-up to prevent relapse.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of infection and gut dysfunction in acute wasting.
Learn energy requirements for recovery in children aged 6–59 months with moderate wasting.
Identify clinical signs of unresolved illness or inflammation in malnourished children.
Explore WASH strategies to mitigate environmental enteric dysfunction (EED).
key_facts: Acute wasting results from a mismatch between intake/absorption and physiological needs, amplified by infections and inflammation.
Environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) causes intestinal inflammation, impaired absorption, and barrier disruption.
Children aged 6–59 months with moderate wasting require 100–130 kcal/kg/day during recovery, per 2023 WHO guidelines.
Relapse after discharge often reflects unresolved infection burden, food insecurity, or caregiving constraints.
Persistent poor appetite is a clinical marker of unresolved illness or inflammation, not behavioral issues.
reference_quote: Immune dysfunction as a cause and consequence of malnutrition — https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK601642/
